evidence.claim: Append-log designs should use append-only sequential records with clear commit semantics; WAL designs append changes to a separate log and can allow readers and writers to proceed concurrently.
evidence.support: WAL changes are appended to a separate file; commit occurs when a commit record is appended; WAL allows concurrent reads/writes and more sequential disk I/O.

evidence.claim: Durability is a configurable performance tradeoff; fsync improves durability but slows commits.
evidence.support: When WAL is enabled, default commits issue fsync to help ensure data reaches disk; this improves durability but slows commits.

evidence.claim: Structured logging and consistent event attributes are required for operational/security use; logs support audit trails and incident reconstruction.
evidence.support: Application logging should be consistent and includes audit trails, policy violations, incident investigation, and security monitoring.

evidence.claim: Log injection/forging is a concrete threat when unvalidated input is written to logs.
evidence.support: Writing invalidated user input to log files can allow forged entries or malicious content injection.

evidence.claim: Implementation should use low-level append/open semantics rather than seek-then-write patterns; Python exposes os.open and O_APPEND for append-on-each-write behavior.
evidence.support: Python os provides O_APPEND and open/write/fsync primitives for OS-level file operations.
